title: Dirac Notation Basics
objective: Read and write the bra-ket language that quantum mechanics uses to represent states, operators, and measurement relationships.
beginnerExplanation:
Dirac notation is a compact way of writing vectors and inner products. A quantum state is written as a ket, like |psi>. Its dual is a bra, like <psi|. When you place a bra and ket together, you get an inner product, which tells you about overlap and probability amplitude.

At first this looks abstract, but it is just linear algebra written in a way that fits quantum problems cleanly. The notation makes it easier to talk about states, operators, basis changes, and tensor products without carrying large matrices around all the time.
deeperExplanation:
A ket is a column vector in a complex Hilbert space. A bra is its conjugate transpose. Operators act on kets from the left, and expectation values look like <psi|A|psi>. Measurement probabilities in a chosen basis come from the squared magnitude of the inner products between the state and basis vectors.

Dirac notation also makes tensor products and multi-system reasoning easier to read. For example, |01> means the tensor product |0> tensor |1>. This is one reason the notation appears everywhere from introductory quantum circuits to advanced many-body theory.
keyTakeaways: Bra-ket notation is linear algebra written in quantum-friendly form.
Kets represent states and bras represent their conjugate duals.
Inner products encode overlap and measurement amplitude.
The notation scales naturally to operators and multi-qubit systems.
example: If a qubit is in the state |psi> = (|0> + |1>)/sqrt(2), then the amplitude of measuring 0 is <0|psi> = 1/sqrt(2), and the probability is the squared magnitude: 1/2.
commonMistakes: Treating bras and kets as unrelated symbols instead of dual objects.
Forgetting the complex conjugate when moving from ket to bra.
exercise: Write the state |psi> = (|0> - |1>)/sqrt(2) in ordinary vector form and compute the probability of measuring |1>.
